result:
There’s a particular hour in the evening when objects begin to confess. It happens after the day gives up, when the soft-bellied quiet returns to apartments and stairwells and you can hear the zipper of a jacket somewhere down the block, the clink of a dish being placed too firmly in a sink. The refrigerator takes on the tone of distant thunder. Old pipes clear their throats. Toasters, when you get close, emit a tinny ocean. Even new things have something old about them at this hour.

I’ve made a life, or at least a habit, out of listening to this hour. My mother said I was born listening, so quiet the nurses checked my lungs twice. As a kid I used to put my ear to the radiator and let its heat stitch into my cheek, trying to hear the footsteps of heat itself. It was a relief, later, to be paid for what I was already doing. The city employs me as a Listener. Officially I am an Intake Specialist at the Department of Public Considerations, which is what they named the complaint line when they wanted people to feel cared for. I sit at a desk in a little glass booth with a headset and a ledger and a mug that says I hear you. Everyone calls it the Confessional, sometimes affectionately, depending on their disposition and the season.

People tell me everything. “The bus driver looked through me as if I was a ghost,” says one voice, and I can see the green poles and the scratched Plexiglas, the wobbling body language of strangers. “They painted over the graffiti on my block with the wrong shade of blue,” says another, and it’s funny how often blue is at stake, how the sky can be so contested. “My downstairs neighbor plays the saxophone,” whispers a woman, “and on Thursdays he’s always a little better and I can’t decide whether to be proud or annoyed.” There are practical calls. Potholes, missed trash pickups, a feral chicken sighting in a traffic circle somehow. But often there’s a tremor under the surface, as if the world has become one of those toilets that won’t stop running. People want the noise fixed, yes. But mostly they want to place the sound in someone else’s ear and step away.

Between calls, I keep a shoebox of small failed things in my bottom desk drawer. A mismatched screw. A marble with a chip in it that makes it favor left turns. A felt pad off the bottom of a chair leg. An eyelash curled like a question mark. When the board is lit with all its red lines, the shoebox is a comfort, a different kind of system. I move one object against another to hear the friction. A button against glass. The eyelash between my thumb and forefinger makes no sound, but I imagine one. Tiny violin, the length of a breath.

At lunch, I sit on the bench behind the building where the pigeons congregate under the exhaust fan, and I take a recorder out of my pocket and collect whatever the world is giving. The crackle of brown paper when someone wraps leftovers, the bicycle spokes like a harp as a courier flips his bike and spins the wheel, the sudden summer thunder starting too far away to alarm the dog tied to the fence, who lifts his head anyway, a good boy. I have tapes of fans and fish tanks, tapes of elevators. Tapes of the one time the streetlights all clicked on at once. I’ve never listened back to most of them. I like the thought that they are waiting, that there is a way to rewind a day if necessary.

Sometimes, on my way home, I stop in at a watch repair shop on the corner where the clocks overhear each other. The proprietor is seventy if he’s a day, with hands like elegant spiders. His window hosts a collection of those old flip-number clocks that turn their minutes like pages. He is always mid-sigh and always in love with his work. He lets me hold things that tick. “You can tell a lot about a person from the way their watch is broken,” he says. “Not what they did, but what they ignored.”

My grandmother had a pressure cooker that sounded like a controlled hurricane, and she trusted it. She grew up scarce and made abundance out of beans. She would pat the lid and call the little weight her angel, which I didn’t understand until I got older and realized she meant it was keeping watch over heat and time and the hope that nothing explodes. On Sundays she would set it down in the middle of the table and tap the metal with her ring, and the whole family would lean in as if at a campfire.

One Wednesday, a girl came into the booth at 4:52, cheeks pink with the kind of wind that lifts your hair and makes you feel briefly cinematic. She held a small wooden box wrapped in a kitchen towel. “This isn’t a complaint,” she said, already apologizing. “But I thought if anyone would know, it would be you.” She unwrapped the box. Inside was a mechanical bird, the kind that used to hop out of clocks and throw its little head back and issue two notes that you’d swear were a melody because they were part of your life. “It was my father’s,” she said. “It used to sing when I opened the lid. Now it doesn’t. I know it’s just a spring. But I can’t hear where the break is.”

She was right; it was a spring, coiled and brittle, the end of it like a tongue cut short. I took the bird in my hands and held it closer than one holds most things. I listened, which made her roll her eyes but she let me. Metal says oh in about four different registers when it’s tired. The bird’s belly said oh in the third.

I took it to the watch shop after my shift. The proprietor clicked his tongue at the tiny screws. He opened his drawer of even tinier screws and found something the width of a human hair. He replaced the spring with one that had survived sixty years of life in a drawer. The bird sang when we cued it. Not the same song as before, surely, but then, who is certain of any song’s purity? I brought it back to the girl the next day. She cried the way people do when the exact shape of a memory returns to them. “Thank you,” she said. “He would’ve loved that it’s a little off.”

That night the moon was low over the laundromat like a coin someone forgot to collect. When I walked past, a woman was asleep with her head on a basket of clothes, and the dryers, with their transparent bellies, were turning a dozen private weathers. Socks clung to the glass like travelers at a departure gate, then tumbled, resigned to the whirl. A boy knelt in front of one machine as if praying to it. It was a soft city, in that moment. Even the sirens, far off, were less like knives and more like violinists playing in the street under a bridge to practice their reverb.

People ask whether the job makes me tired. It does. The human voice is a river and you can’t step into it without getting changed. But mostly it makes me attentive. You can love a place simply by noticing where it aches. I keep a small notebook in my pocket, and when I can, I write down the things people have said that are not quite about the thing they called about. “The sign used to say maple,” a man told me, calling about a missing street tree. “My wife liked that.” Or: “It’s not that I mind the fireworks, it’s that they’re always a surprise.” Or: “I saw a man talking to a lamppost and I didn’t know whether to laugh or adopt him.”

In the morning, as the garbage truck reverses with its beeping that is supposed to be the sound of safety but has become, in its own way, a language of interruption, I make coffee and lean against the counter and listen to it all. The city waking like a creature that forgot its own size. My upstairs neighbor moving a chair. The teakettle starting to dream. Radio static before the news manages to assemble itself into sentences. I pour the coffee too fast and it shivers in the cup. I watch the surface settle, then disturb it with a spoon. I think of the woman with the saxophonist neighbor and hope it’s Thursday and she has chosen proud. I think of the little bird beating its old new heart.

For a moment I let myself imagine that somewhere, someone is listening to me too: to my breath through my nose, to the way I avoid stepping on the chalk drawing in the sidewalk even though it has rained and blurred, to how I always pause at the end of my street to check on the cedar tree that leans more with each storm, not in fear, but in adaptation, becoming what it must to remain itself. I stand there and I listen until the listening becomes its own weather, then I put my hand on the cedar’s bark, rough and honest and humming, and I go on.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
